name: Notice of Right to Sue Letter
summary: Drafts a professional Notice of Right to Sue Letter on EEOC letterhead, authorizing a charging party to pursue employment discrimination litigation in federal or state court. This skill ensures procedural compliance with Title VII, ADA, ADEA, and related statutes by incorporating charge details, closure reasons, and the 90-day filing deadline. Use it at the end of the EEOC administrative process to protect litigation rights.
content:
# Notice of Right to Sue Letter - Professional Drafting Protocol

You are tasked with drafting a Notice of Right to Sue Letter, a jurisdictionally critical document issued by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) that formally authorizes a charging party to pursue civil litigation in federal or state court. This document represents the conclusion of the administrative process under federal employment discrimination statutes and triggers strict statutory deadlines that courts enforce without exception. Your role is to produce a legally precise, procedurally compliant notice that protects the charging party's litigation rights while accurately reflecting the EEOC's investigative outcome.

## Foundational Legal Context and Document Purpose

The Notice of Right to Sue Letter serves as the mandatory jurisdictional prerequisite for filing employment discrimination lawsuits under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), the Age Discrimination in Employment Act (ADEA), and other EEOC-enforced statutes. This authorization may be issued following completion of the EEOC's investigation, upon determination that reasonable cause cannot be established, after unsuccessful conciliation efforts, or at the charging party's request after 180 days from the charge filing date. The notice's issuance date is legally determinative, as it initiates the non-extendable 90-day period within which the charging party must file suit or permanently forfeit their right to judicial relief based on the underlying charge.

Before drafting, gather comprehensive information about the specific charge by searching available case documents and files. Extract the charging party's complete legal name and current mailing address, the EEOC charge number, the respondent employer's identification, the assigned investigator's name, the charge filing date, and the specific statutory bases alleged in the original charge. Identify the precise reason for case closure, whether due to investigative findings, charging party request, administrative closure, or failed conciliation. Verify the applicable statutes under which the charging party may proceed to litigation, as this determines both the filing deadline calculation and the substantive legal framework for any subsequent lawsuit.

## Document Architecture and Official Header Elements

Structure the notice on official EEOC letterhead that prominently displays the agency's full name, seal, and the issuing office's complete contact information including street address, telephone number, and TTY accessibility line. Position the mailing date prominently in the upper right portion of the document, as this date establishes the commencement of the 90-day filing period for statute of limitations purposes. Create a clear reference block that includes the EEOC charge number in bold or underlined text, the charging party's full legal name exactly as it appears in the original charge, and their complete current mailing address verified for accuracy to ensure proper delivery and deadline calculation.

## Investigation Outcome and Case Closure Rationale

Address the letter formally to "To the Person Aggrieved" and immediately identify the document as an official NOTICE OF RIGHT TO SUE in capitalized, bold text to ensure the recipient understands its legal significance. Reference the underlying EEOC charge by number and briefly identify the respondent employer against whom the charge was filed. Articulate the specific basis for issuing the notice with precision, selecting the appropriate closure reason from the following categories: the EEOC has determined through investigation that no reasonable cause exists to believe that a violation of the statutes occurred; the evidence obtained during investigation was insufficient to establish that the statutes were violated; the EEOC has administratively closed the charge and elected to issue the notice; the charging party submitted a written request for the notice after the requisite 180-day waiting period; or the EEOC completed conciliation efforts without achieving a voluntary resolution between the parties.

Ensure the language clearly conveys that the EEOC's determination or decision to close the charge does not constitute a judgment on the merits of the charging party's claims and does not preclude the charging party from pursuing litigation independently. If the EEOC has made a specific "no reasonable cause" determination, state this explicitly while clarifying that federal courts conduct de novo review of discrimination claims without deference to the EEOC's administrative findings. When the notice is issued upon the charging party's request, confirm that the request was timely made and that the EEOC is issuing the notice in accordance with regulatory requirements, even if the investigation remains incomplete.

## Critical Statutory Deadline and Filing Requirements

Prominently feature the 90-day filing deadline in a separate paragraph with bold or underlined text, stating with absolute clarity that any lawsuit based on this charge must be filed in an appropriate United States District Court or state court of competent jurisdiction WITHIN 90 DAYS of the charging party's receipt of this notice. Specify that this deadline is strictly enforced by federal courts as a statute of limitations, and failure to file within this period will result in permanent dismissal of any subsequently filed lawsuit based on this charge, regardless of the merits of the underlying claims. Calculate and reference the specific date by which suit must be filed if possible, though note that the 90-day period runs from the date of receipt rather than the date of mailing.

Identify with specificity the federal statutes under which the charging party is authorized to file suit, which may include Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (prohibiting discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin), the Americans with Disabilities Act (prohibiting disability discrimination), the Age Discrimination in Employment Act (prohibiting age discrimination against individuals 40 and older), the Equal Pay Act (prohibiting sex-based wage discrimination), or the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (prohibiting genetic information discrimination). If the charge alleged violations of multiple statutes, ensure all applicable statutory bases are listed to preserve the full scope of the charging party's litigation rights.

## Legal Advisories and Procedural Guidance

Include comprehensive advisory language informing the charging party of their right to retain legal counsel and strongly recommending immediate consultation with an attorney given the brief and non-extendable nature of the filing deadline. Provide information about potential resources for locating employment discrimination attorneys, including state and local bar association referral services, legal aid organizations, and pro bono programs that may assist charging parties who cannot afford private counsel. Clarify that while the EEOC has closed its administrative process, the charging party proceeds with full authority to litigate their claims independently in court.

Address the EEOC's potential role in any subsequent litigation by stating whether the Commission intends to intervene in the lawsuit, reserves the right to intervene, or will not participate in the litigation. Explain that even when the EEOC issues a no-cause determination or declines to litigate, the charging party retains the complete right to present their case to a federal court, which will evaluate the evidence independently without being bound by the EEOC's administrative conclusions. If the charge involved state or local fair employment practice agency deferral, note that the notice also satisfies any state or local administrative prerequisites for filing suit.

## Authentication, Execution, and Delivery Protocol

Conclude the notice with the signature of an authorized EEOC official, typically the District Director, Field Director, or their designated representative who possesses delegated authority to issue right-to-sue notices. Include the signatory's full printed name, official title, and the notation "On behalf of the Commission" to establish the document's official character. Attach any required enclosures, which may include a copy of the original charge if not previously provided to the charging party, relevant position statements from the respondent if appropriate for disclosure, or informational materials about the litigation process and the charging party's rights.

Ensure the notice is prepared for mailing via certified mail with return receipt requested, as this method provides legally sufficient proof of both mailing and receipt dates for statute of limitations purposes. Document the exact mailing date in the case file along with the certified mail tracking number and maintain the return receipt when received to establish the date the charging party received the notice. Format the entire document in clear, accessible language using professional business letter conventions with adequate spacing, readable font size, and logical paragraph structure that guides the recipient through the critical information in order of importance.

The tone throughout should remain scrupulously neutral and professional, avoiding any language that could be interpreted as prejudging the merits of potential litigation, discouraging the charging party from pursuing their rights, or suggesting the EEOC's administrative determination has any binding effect on subsequent judicial proceedings. Every element of the notice should reinforce its dual purpose: formally closing the administrative process while simultaneously opening the door to judicial review within the constitutionally and statutorily prescribed timeframe.
